content.text: Beyond the GPU: The Accelerator Landscape

NVIDIA GPUs dominate LLM workloads, but a growing ecosystem of purpose-built accelerators offers real tradeoffs: lower latency, lower cost per inference token, or purpose-built training efficiency. Understanding when each hardware type fits requires knowing its memory architecture, compute model, and ecosystem maturity.
The table above surfaces the key architectural axes: memory hierarchy (HBM vs SRAM-only), interconnect, latency profile, and ecosystem depth. Each axis maps to different deployment scenarios.
Google TPU: Systolic Arrays and Scale
Each TPU chip contains one or more

— 2D systolic arrays of multiply-accumulate cells. Data flows in from the left edge and weight values stream down from the top, producing the dot-product for one output cell per clock. This removes the register-file traffic that limits CPU/GPU throughput for dense matrix multiplies.
TPU v5p connects up to 8,960 chips via
, forming a 3D torus mesh with 4.8 Tb/s aggregate all-reduce bandwidth per chip — enabling pipeline-parallel training across thousands of chips without resorting to Ethernet.
MXU operates in BF16 accumulating to FP32. BF16 has the same exponent width as FP32, so dynamic range is preserved without loss-scaling complexity.
TensorFlow/JAX programs are compiled by XLA into a static computation graph. Runtime dispatch overhead is eliminated, but any Python-level dynamism must be traced away.
TPU v5p has 95 GB HBM2e per chip at 2.76 TB/s bandwidth — comparable to H100 PCIe but lower than H100 SXM5.

# JAX on TPU — minimal Llama inference
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
import os

# Set JAX to target TPU (in Cloud TPU VM or Colab TPU runtime)
# os.environ["JAX_PLATFORM_NAME"] = "tpu"

# jit + pmap distribute across TPU cores
@jax.pmap
def forward_step(params, tokens):
    # Each shard handles its own slice of the batch
    logits = model.apply(params, tokens)  # jax.flax model
    return logits

# Replicate parameters across devices
params_replicated = jax.device_put_replicated(params, jax.devices())
# Shard batch across devices
tokens_sharded = tokens.reshape(jax.device_count(), -1, seq_len)

logits = forward_step(params_replicated, tokens_sharded)

repo demonstrate efficient TPU serving. The key constraint: XLA re-compilation triggers whenever input shape changes, so you must pad to fixed shapes or use batched decode with uniform sequence lengths.
AWS's custom silicon splits the workload:
(inf2 instances) targets inference. Both are based on NeuronCore-v2 compute tiles.
Matrix multiply for BF16/FP8/INT8. The trn2 chip delivers 3.1 PFLOPS BF16.
800 Gb/s chip-to-chip interconnect for tensor parallelism across chips within an instance.
) or TensorFlow models to a static Neuron Executable Format (NEFF) artifact. Runtime execution replays the compiled plan without re-tracing:

# Install: pip install torch-neuronx neuronx-cc transformers-neuronx
import torch
import torch_neuronx
from transformers_neuronx import LlamaForSampling

# Load and compile for 2-chip tensor parallelism, bs=4
model = LlamaForSampling.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf",
    batch_size=4,
    tp_degree=2,   # NeuronLink across 2 NeuronCores
    amp='bf16',
)
model.to_neuron()  # triggers neuronx-cc compilation (~10–30 min)

# Serialise compiled artifact so subsequent launches skip compilation
model.save("llama-7b-bs4-tp2.neff")

# Inference (reload from artifact on later starts)
# model = LlamaForSampling.from_pretrained(...); model.load("...")
with torch.inference_mode():
    input_ids = torch.zeros((4, 128), dtype=torch.int32)
    outputs = model.sample(input_ids, sequence_length=512)
The NEFF file is hardware-revision specific — regenerate it when upgrading instance type. The
compiler applies operator fusion, quantisation, and layout optimisation automatically during compilation.
(12 Inferentia2 chips, 384 GB HBM) costs roughly
on-demand, vs ~$32/hr for a comparable p4d.24xlarge (8× A100 80GB). For static-shape serving workloads, the compilation overhead amortises within minutes of sustained traffic.
Groq's Language Processing Unit (LPU) is built on the

— a dataflow architecture that eliminates caches, branch predictors, and dynamic scheduling. The compiler determines the exact memory address, compute unit, and clock cycle for every operation at compile time.

: every request for the same model and sequence length takes exactly the same number of cycles, with zero jitter. This contrasts sharply with GPUs where DRAM prefetch misses, cache conflicts, and warp divergence introduce latency variance.
230 MB on-chip SRAM with ~80 TB/s bandwidth. No DRAM — the entire compiled model weights live on-chip.
A GroqRack (576 chips) has enough SRAM to hold 70B-parameter models. Chips are connected via a Groq-proprietary ChipLink with ~900 Gb/s per direction.
Only models compiled for the LPU can run. Custom architectures require re-compilation and validation by Groq.

# pip install groq
from groq import Groq

client = Groq()  # uses GROQ_API_KEY env var

# Streaming completion — deterministic token-to-token latency
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="llama-3.1-70b-versatile",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain attention in transformers."}],
    stream=True,
    max_tokens=512,
)

import time
first_token_ts = None
for chunk in response:
    if chunk.choices[0].delta.content:
        if first_token_ts is None:
            first_token_ts = time.perf_counter()
        print(chunk.choices[0].delta.content, end="", flush=True)

# Groq publishes p99 TTFT < 0.4s and inter-token latency < 1ms
Groq's GroqCloud API is OpenAI-compatible, so any application targeting
can switch providers by changing the base URL and API key.
Intel Gaudi: Open Ecosystem at Scale
Intel Gaudi 3 (formerly Habana Labs) uses two engine types on each chip:
8× systolic array blocks for GEMM in BF16/FP8. Gaudi 3 peaks at 1835 TFLOPS BF16 — approaching H100 SXM5 (1979 TFLOPS BF16).
32 programmable VLIW SIMD processors for element-wise, reduce, and custom ops. TPC kernels are written in a C-like language.
More VRAM than H100 SXM5 (80 GB), at higher bandwidth — useful for serving large models without tensor parallelism.
Gaudi 3 replaces NVLink with commodity Ethernet. For scale-out training this adds latency vs NVLink, but allows standard datacenter networking.

# pip install optimum[habana] transformers
from optimum.habana import GaudiConfig, GaudiTrainer, GaudiTrainingArguments
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-hf")

gaudi_config = GaudiConfig(use_fused_adam=True, use_fused_clip_norm=True)

training_args = GaudiTrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./output",
    use_habana=True,
    use_lazy_mode=True,   # defers HPU graph execution for better fusion
    gaudi_config_name="Habana/llama",
    per_device_train_batch_size=8,
    bf16=True,
    num_train_epochs=3,
)

trainer = GaudiTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=train_ds,
    gaudi_config=gaudi_config,
)
trainer.train()
batches HPU graph submissions and replays them. For models that don't change shape between steps this eliminates Python dispatch overhead, similar to
Cerebras WSE: Wafer-Scale for Extreme Bandwidth

The Cerebras Wafer Scale Engine 3 (WSE-3) is a single silicon die the size of an entire 300mm wafer — 7 nm, 4 trillion transistors, 900,000 AI-optimised cores, and 44 GB of on-chip SRAM with an effective bandwidth of around 21 PB/s. For comparison, H100 has 80 GB HBM3 with 3.35 TB/s — roughly 6,000× lower bandwidth to weight memory.
The tradeoff: models must fit entirely in 44 GB of on-chip SRAM. The 7B parameter Llama at FP16 takes ~14 GB and fits. A 70B model does not. Cerebras CS-3 systems add
— a large external DRAM bank that streams weights to the WSE — but this narrows the bandwidth advantage significantly.

# Cerebras Model Zoo (open-sourced at github.com/Cerebras/modelzoo)
# Run on Cerebras Cloud Service (CCS)
import cerebras.pytorch as cstorch
import torch

cs_config = cstorch.CSConfig(
    num_csx=1,       # number of CS-3 systems
    max_act_per_csx=1,
)

# Wrap a standard PyTorch model
compiled_model = cstorch.compile(model, backend="CSX")

# Standard PyTorch training loop — cstorch intercepts .backward()
for batch in dataloader:
    outputs = compiled_model(**batch)
    loss = outputs.loss
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Accelerator
LLM decode with batch size 1 has arithmetic intensity ≈ 1 FLOP per byte of weight loaded — the
regime. Any accelerator with higher memory bandwidth delivers faster decode. SRAM-based designs (Groq, Cerebras) exploit this by eliminating DRAM latency entirely.

def roofline_toks_per_sec(
    param_count: int,
    dtype_bytes: int = 2,     # BF16
    mem_bandwidth_tbs: float = 3.35,  # TB/s (H100 SXM5)
    batch_size: int = 1,
) -> float:
    """Estimate decode throughput (memory-bandwidth limited)."""
    weight_bytes = param_count * dtype_bytes
    # At bs=1: one forward pass loads all weights once
    seconds_per_token = weight_bytes / (mem_bandwidth_tbs * 1e12)
    tokens_per_second = batch_size / seconds_per_token
    return tokens_per_second

# H100 SXM5, Llama 2 70B FP16
h100 = roofline_toks_per_sec(70e9, 2, 3.35, batch_size=1)
# Groq GroqRack (576 chips, ~80 TB/s chip SRAM BW aggregate)
groq = roofline_toks_per_sec(70e9, 2, 80.0, batch_size=1)

print(f"H100 theoretical: {h100:.0f} tok/s")  # ≈ 48 tok/s
print(f"Groq theoretical: {groq:.0f} tok/s")  # ≈ 1143 tok/s
# Real Groq delivers ~800 tok/s — achieves ~70% of bandwidth-roofline
Before committing to a non-NVIDIA accelerator, validate each item:
Run model through the vendor's compiler (neuronx-cc, XLA, Habana bridge) on a stub input and confirm all ops are supported. Unsupported ops fall back to CPU silently — detect with profiling.
Confirm whether variable sequence lengths require padding to fixed shapes or multiple compiled artifacts. Measure padding overhead at your actual length distribution.
Verify INT8/FP8 calibration pipelines exist for your model family. AWS Neuron and Gaudi both support AutoGPTQ workflows, but may require manual layer mappings.
Run your actual traffic mix (requests per second, length distribution) under the target hardware. Roofline estimates assume 100% bandwidth utilisation; real systems achieve 40–80%.
AWS Neuron compilation for 70B models can take 30–60 minutes. Budget build-pipeline time and test rollback procedures.
Confirm FlashAttention, PEFT, DeepSpeed, and any custom CUDA kernels your codebase uses have ported equivalents or acceptable fallbacks.
Groq LPU has no dynamic dispatch; you cannot change generation parameters at runtime once compiled. Cerebras has no HBM for weight overflow; model size is a hard limit.
AWS Inferentia and Google TPU compiled artifacts are version-pinned. SDK upgrades invalidate cached NEFF/XLA artifacts — integrate re-compilation into CI.
Confusing peak TFLOPS with serving throughput:
A TPU v5p's 459 TFLOPS BF16 measures training; decode throughput is memory-bandwidth limited (2.76 TB/s), not compute-limited.
Groq's sub-millisecond per-token latency is real, but GroqCloud rate limits and queue depth at scale change the p99 latency significantly.
Intel Gaudi's 200 GbE RDMA vs NVLink can add 20–40% all-reduce overhead in large-scale training runs vs H100 NVLink-4 configurations.

Non-NVIDIA accelerators unlock real advantages — Groq's deterministic sub-millisecond decode, AWS Inferentia2's cost-per-token, TPU's petascale training efficiency — but each comes with constraints: fixed shapes, limited ecosystem depth, or model-size ceilings. The roofline model explains why SRAM-based chips dominate decode latency: bandwidth, not compute, is the bottleneck at batch size 1. Evaluate alternatives with a production traffic replay, not peak-spec comparisons.
